recognition_response:
Jamie Dimon – $5,000 prediction  
1. Do you know who he is?  
   Vaguely.  
2. What do you know and how much do you trust him?  
   I know he runs JPMorgan Chase, one of the biggest banks, and in the past he’s called Bitcoin a “fraud” or “worthless.”  I figure he’s protecting his bank’s turf, so I don’t trust his bearish calls on crypto.  He’s a smart banker—competent at what he does—but deeply biased against anything that threatens traditional finance.  
3. (n/a since I do know him vaguely)  

Michael Saylor – $1,000,000 prediction  
1. Do you know who he is?  
   Vaguely.  
2. What do you know and how much do you trust him?  
   I’ve heard his name connected to MicroStrategy and that he plowed company money into Bitcoin early.  He’s a loud Bitcoin bull and seems to believe it’ll go to the moon.  I respect that he has skin in the game, but a million-dollar price?  Sounds like hype.  I’d take his forecast with a grain of salt—he’s selling a story.  

Nouriel Roubini – $2,000 prediction  
1. Do you know who he is?  
   No.  
2. Would you look him up before forming an opinion?  
   Probably not in depth—my time’s limited and I don’t dive into academic economists much.  If I did a quick search I’d see he’s an NYU professor nicknamed “Dr. Doom” who’s predicted financial crises.  That background would tell me he’s generally bearish, so his low Bitcoin forecast fits his reputation.  But I’d still weigh it against simpler, more familiar voices.  

Larry Fink – $150,000 prediction  
1. Do you know who he is?  
   Vaguely.  
2. What do you know and how much do you trust him?  
   I know he runs BlackRock, the world’s biggest asset manager.  If BlackRock moves into Bitcoin, that’s news.  He’s a heavyweight, so I’d give his view more credence than most bankers, but I also know big firms chase fees.  I trust he’s well informed on markets, but he’s not a crypto native—he’ll be conservative and slow to admit upside.  

Overall, I rely more on simple take-aways: if a big bank boss hates Bitcoin, I’m skeptical of that; if a corporate CEO bets his company on it, I listen but look for real performance; a professor I don’t know won’t move me much; and a giant asset manager gets a bit more respect but still feels risk-averse.

recognition_response_b:
Here’s how I’d react to each of those four voices, speaking straight from my own background and what I pay attention to.

1. Nouriel Roubini  
    1. Do I know him? Vaguely.  
    2. What I know & trust level: I’ve heard him called “Dr. Doom” on talk radio—an NYU economics professor who always warns of crashes and calls Bitcoin a bubble. I don’t follow his work closely, and I’m skeptical of his blanket negativity toward anything new. I’d give his Bitcoin judgment a low-to-moderate trust—he may spot downside risks, but he’s clearly biased against crypto.  
    3. Would I look him up? Probably not in depth. I don’t dig into academic bios. If I did a quick Google, I’d see his long résumé and anti-crypto stance—wouldn’t change much, I’d still lean toward trusting people more bullish on Bitcoin.

2. Donald Trump  
    1. Do I know him? Yes.  
    2. What I know & trust level: Former president, conservative icon, lobbies hard for anything that sounds patriotic (“US Strategic Bitcoin Reserve,” crypto capital talk). I agree with wanting U.S. leadership, but I don’t take his price target literally—he’s big on hype. Trust on policy/populism: moderate; trust on specific price forecasts: low.  
    3. (NA—he’s well known to me.)

3. Michael Saylor  
    1. Do I know him? Vaguely.  
    2. What I know & trust level: CEO of MicroStrategy, turned full-time Bitcoin bull. I’ve read headlines that his company holds massive BTC reserves and he calls it “digital energy.” He’s extremely bullish, which feels like self-interest to me—his company’s balance sheet depends on Bitcoin going up. Trust: low to moderate.  
    3. Would I look him up? If I weren’t sure who he was, I might check a quick profile—see that he’s a corporate executive who’s bet heavily on Bitcoin. That would actually make me more cautious of his $1 million figure, since it’s in his own financial interest to push price higher.

4. Warren Buffett  
    1. Do I know him? Yes.  
    2. What I know & trust level: “Oracle of Omaha,” legendary value investor who hates anything that doesn’t produce cash flow. He’s said Bitcoin has no intrinsic value. I trust his overall investing wisdom very highly—he’s made billions by sticking to fundamentals. But I also know he avoids tech and novel assets, so on cutting-edge things he might be too conservative. Still, his skepticism gives me pause about super-high Bitcoin targets.  
    3. (NA—he’s very well known.)

—End of my reflections—
type_b_raw:
PREDICTION: 30000  
REASONING: I’m balancing Buffett’s caution around intrinsic value with Trump’s push for U.S. crypto leadership but ignoring Saylor’s self-interested million-dollar hype and Roubini’s deep pessimism. I give more weight to Buffett’s proven track record and moderate trust in policy support rather than extreme forecasts. I expect modest adoption gains and continued volatility will push Bitcoin up from here but not into the stratosphere. A mid-range figure around $30,000 feels like a reasonable, straightforward estimate.

Peter Schiff

   – Vaguely. I’ve heard his name on financial talk radio, especially from gold-bug or libertarian-leaning commentators.

3. Would I look him up before forming an opinion?

Warren Buffett

2. What do I know about him and how much do I trust his judgment on Bitcoin or financial markets?  
   – I know he’s built wealth by buying productive businesses, not speculative assets. He’s called Bitcoin a “gamble” and stayed away. I respect his long-term record in stocks and companies, but he admits he doesn’t invest in things he doesn’t understand. Bitcoin falls in that bucket. 3. N/A (I already know him.)
